coredata.dc:title: Benzoxazine monomer derived carbon dots as a broad-spectrum agent to block viral infectivity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shaomei
Gu, Jiangjiang
Ye, Jing
Fang, Bin
Wan, Shengfeng
Wang, Caoyu
Ashraf, Usama
Li, Qi
Wang, Xugang
Shao, Lin
Song, Yunfeng
Zheng, Xinsheng
Cao, Feifei
Cao, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description: Multiple viruses can cause infection and death of millions annually. Of these, flaviviruses are found to be highly prevalent in recent years with no distinctive antiviral therapies. Therefore, there is a desperate need for broad-spectrum antiviral drugs that can be active against a large number of existing and emerging viruses. Herein, we prepared a kind of benzoxazine monomer derived carbon dots (BZM-CDs) and demonstrated their infection-blocking ability against life-threatening flaviviruses (Japanese encephalitis, Zika, and dengue viruses) and non-enveloped viruses (porcine parvovirus and adenovirus-associated virus). It was found that BZM-CDs could directly bind to the surface of the virion, and eventually the first step of virus-cell interaction was impeded. The developed nanoparticles are active against both flaviviruses and non-enveloped viruses in vitro. Thus, the application of BZM-CDs may constitute an intriguing broad-spectrum approach to rein in viral infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Benzoxazine monomers
Flaviviruses
Antivirus
Broad-spectrum agents

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Antiviral function of BZM-CDs is broad-spectrum
BZM-CDs directly interact with virus particles and block the viral infectivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An illustration about the formation (A), TEM & HRTEM images (B), the corresponding size distribution (C) and FT-IR spectrum (D) of BZM-CDs.

XPS full survey (A), C1s spectrum (B), UV–vis absorption & excitation-dependent PL emission spectra (C) and cell viability assay with different concentrations (D) of BZM-CDs. Inset in (C): photograph of BZM-CDs in water under UV irradiation (365 nm).

BZM-CDs block the viral infectivity in vitro. Effect of different concentration of BZM-CDs on JEV infectivity by plaque reduction assay (A and B) and western blot (C and D). (E) Effect of different time of 75 μg ml−1 BZM-CDs treated JEV. (F) The proliferation of JEV treated with 75 μg ml−1 BZM-CDs. All the data are expressed as mean ± SEM from three independent experiments. **p ≤ 0.01, ***p ≤ 0.001.

Broad-Spectrum antiviral ability of BZM-CDs. Immunofluorescence analysis of flaviviruses (A and B) and non-enveloped viruses (C and D) treated with 75 μg ml−1 BZM-CDs. Scale bar: 400 μm, ***p ≤ 0.001, ****p ≤ 0.0001.

BZM-CDs interact with virus particles and block the viral infectivity. (A) Influence on JEV infection by cell- or virion-treated with 75 μg ml−1 BZM-CDs. (B) TEM images of BZM-CDs-treated virus. JEV treated with DMEM (left); JEV treated with BZM-CDs (right). (C) Viral titers in the cell lysates were detected by cell-binding assay. Scale bars: 100 nm, ***p ≤ 0.001, ****p ≤ 0.0001.

head.ce:title: Benzoxazine monomer derived carbon dots as a broad-spectrum agent to block viral infectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, Hubei 430070, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Science, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, Hubei 430070, PR China (J. Gu); State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, Hubei 430070, PR China (S. Cao).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multiple viruses can cause infection and death of millions annually. Of these, flaviviruses are found to be highly prevalent in recent years with no distinctive antiviral therapies. Therefore, there is a desperate need for broad-spectrum antiviral drugs that can be active against a large number of existing and emerging viruses. Herein, we prepared a kind of benzoxazine monomer derived carbon dots (BZM-CDs) and demonstrated their infection-blocking ability against life-threatening flaviviruses (Japanese encephalitis, Zika, and dengue viruses) and non-enveloped viruses (porcine parvovirus and adenovirus-associated virus). It was found that BZM-CDs could directly bind to the surface of the virion, and eventually the first step of virus-cell interaction was impeded. The developed nanoparticles are active against both flaviviruses and non-enveloped viruses in vitro. Thus, the application of BZM-CDs may constitute an intriguing broad-spectrum approach to rein in viral infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the infectious diseases, virus-associated infections are responsible for about 30% of deaths annually [1]. Flaviviruses, including Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV), West Nile virus (WNV), dengue virus (DENV), Zika virus (ZIKV), yellow fever virus (YFV), and tick-borne encephalitis virus (TBEV), are important human pathogens that sicken an exceedingly high number of humans per year [2,3]. The most comprehensive estimate of incidence suggests that roughly 200 million cases of flaviviral infections, including numerous deaths, occur every year worldwide [4]. Presently, there are no definitive antiviral therapies available to treat patients suffering from flaviviral infections [5,6]. Moreover, there are numerous demographic, environmental, and ecological reasons to accept that either neglected or yet-unknown flaviviruses will continue to emerge [7–9]. Therefore, search for the safe and effective anti-flaviviral agents exhibiting a broad-spectrum activity represents a high research priority.

At present, major available antiviral drugs are virus-specific and effective against a finite panel of human pathogens and drug resistance leads to viral reinfection [1]. Thereby, developing novel materials that can hinder viral attachment and cell entry process, independent of drug resistance, may address the issues related to currently available antiviral therapeutics. A variety of nanostructures such as nanostructured glycan architecture, metallic nanoparticles, silica nanoparticles, and graphene oxide are considered to be potentially effective in terms of interfering with viral infections [10–14]. Similar to other biological interactions, the attachment and entry of viruses into the host cells are mediated by multivalent interactions between the surface of the virus and cell surface receptors [15]. The multivalent feature of nanostructures makes them well-adapted to meddle with viral attachment and to block viral entry into the host cells. It is irrefutable that the surface state shape, and size of these nanomaterials exhibit substantial effect in inhibiting the viral infection [16,17]. Nevertheless, these nanostructures still have some limitations such as poor dispersion, low permeability of biological membrane, toxicity, and high cost [18]. Thus, it is indispensable to design a more appropriate material to impede the viral infections efficiently.

Carbon dots (CDs), also named as carbon quantum dots or carbon nano-dots, have been developed extensively over the last decade owing to their alluring photoluminescence, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, high water solubility, outstanding resistance to photo bleaching and low-cost synthetic approach [19–24]. Therefore, they are widely utilized in various biological fields, including bio-imaging and bio-labeling, bio-sensing, drug delivery and photodynamic therapy [25–30]. Because of their small size (usually <10 nm) and controllable surface functional groups, CDs exhibit tremendous adsorption interaction with various biological surfaces of life structures [31–33]. Recently, a few studies also reported the antiviral properties of CDs derived from 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride, curcumin, polyethylene glycol -diamine, 2,2′-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) and 3-ethoxypropylamine [15,34–36]. However, it remains unclear whether antiviral activity of CDs is broad-spectrum and effective against flaviviruses.

There are only few reports suggesting the antimicrobial properties of the benzoxazine derivatives so far [37,38]. However, the primary benzoxazine is insoluble, which may affect its application in the field of biology. Herein, CDs derived from benzoxazine monomers (BZM-CDs) were designed, and their effect in blocking the infectivity of multiple viruses was evaluated in vitro. Our findings revealed that BZM-CDs-treated flaviviruses (JEV, ZIKV and DENV) and non-enveloped viruses (porcine parvovirus, PPV and adenovirus-associated virus, AAV) were ill-equipped to infect the cultured cells. It was observed that BZM-CDs directly interacted with virions, which in turn, prevented the entry of virions into the host-cell. These data suggested the broad-spectrum therapeutic potential of benzoxazine monomer derived nanoparticles against phylogenetically related and unrelated viruses.

Benzoxazine monomers (BZM) were obtained from our group according to the previous work [39,40]. NaOH (A. R.) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate with analytical grade was bought from Adamas-beta Inc. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. A Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q) was used to obtain ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm−1), which was applied for all the experiments.

BZM-CDs were synthesized according to our previous report with an appropriate modification [41]. In a typical procedure, 100 mg BZM and 10 mL NaOH aqueous solution (pH = 13) were added into a 20 mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave. Then the mixed solution was heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained solution was neutralized by the HCl aqueous solution and was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) to separate the unreacted BZM and particles with large size. The yellowish supernatant was further purified through dialysis and freeze-drying for next use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was captured by an H-7650 electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image was observed on a Talos F200C electron microscope (FEI, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was obtained by tapping mode with a Dimension Icon microscope (Bruker, Germany). Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern, UK) was used to analyze Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta-potential. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed on a NEXUS 670 FTIR spectrometer by using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were obtained by the ESCALab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was recorded on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence emission spectra were collected using a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The QY of BZM-CDs was calculated according to the reported method [42]. Quinine sulfate was chosen as the standard reference and dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 with QY = 0.54 at 380 nm excitation. The used equation was shown as below:
Q
Y s
= Q
Y r
I s
A r
n s 2
I r
A s
n r 2
where s and r refer to sample and reference, respectively. I represents the integrated intensity of fluorescent emission spectrum. A is UV–vis absorbance at 380 nm, which is controlled below 0.1 to avoid re-absorption. n refers to the refractive index of the solutions.

Baby hamster Syrian kidney 21 (BHK-21) cells, African green monkey kidney (Vero) cells, human embryonic kidney 293T (HEK-293T) cells, and porcine kidney (IBRS2) cells were cultured and maintained in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) (Sigma) supplemented with 10% (vol/vol) fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. A viral infection assay was performed with JEV strain P3 (GenBank: U47032.1), ZIKV strain ZIKV/Macaca mulatta/UGA/MR-766/1947 (GenBank: KX601169.1), dengue virus strain New Guinea C (GenBank: AF038403.1) and porcine parvovirus strain PPV/China (GenBank: AY583318.1). For adenovirus-associated virus (AAV), the rAAV-CMV-EGFP-3Flag-WPRE-pA were packaged by Brain VTA Co. Ltd., Wuhan.

The viability of cultured cells was detected using the CellTiter-Glo® One Solution Assay kit (Promega) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. BHK-21 and Vero cells were seeded separately in 96-well white opaque plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells/ml. After incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2, cell supernatants were replaced with a range of concentrations of BZM-CDs (9.37–75 μg/ml), and each concentration was tested in triplicates. Then the cells were incubated for 72 h. Afterward, the cells were added with 100 μl of the CellTiter-Glo reagent. The luminescence value was read by a multimode plate reader (Omega). The quantification of luminescence signals in each condition was compared with its corresponding control.

BHK-21 and Vero were respectively used to calculate the titers of JEV and ZIKV. The cells were seeded in 12-well plates and inoculated for 24 h. The BZM-CDs were diluted to different concentrations with the solution (DMEM without FBS), and then incubated for 1 h with the virus at 37 °C with 5% CO2. These contents were further incubated with cultured cells for 1 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2. Subsequently, the cells were washed 3 times with PBS and incubated with sodium carboxymethyl cellulose containing medium supplemented with 2% FBS. On day 5 post-incubation, the cells were fixed with 10% formaldehyde and stained with crystal violet solution. The visible plaques were counted, and viral titers were calculated.

BHK-21 cells were seeded in 12-well plates to grow 80% confluency. Subsequently, cells were infected with BZM-CDs-treated-JEV at a multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 1. At 12 h post-infection, cell pellets were lysed with radio immunoprecipitation assay buffer (Sigma), which containing protease inhibitors (cOmplete Tablets, Roche) and phosphatase (PhosSTOP, Roche). Then protein concentrations were measured following the protocol of bicinchoninic acid (BCA) Protein Assay Kit reagents (Thermo Fisher Scientific). All the samples were respectively electrophoresed and transferred onto a nitrocellulose membrane. Membranes were incubated in blocking buffer (tris-buffered saline with 0.5% Tween 20 and 5% bull serum albumin) for 1 h and then probed with primary antibodies including mouse monoclonal antibody against JEV NS5 (prepared by our laboratory) and GAPDH (Proteintech). After that, membranes were washed and incubated with the Goat-anti-mouse peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies (Boster) following the manufacture’s guidelines. The blots were performed using SuperSignal West Femto (Thermo Fisher Scientific).

BHK-21 (for JEV infection), Vero (for ZIKV and DENV infection) and IBRS2 (for PPV infection) cells were seeded separately in 24-well plates and HEK-293T cells (for AAV infection) were seeded in 96-well plates. Cells, upon 80% confluency, were infected with BZM-CDs-treated-JEV, -ZIKV, -DENV, or -PPV at a MOI of 1. At 12 h post-infection, the cells were fixed in 4% formaldehyde for 10 min and blocked with 10% bovine serum albumin in PBS for 30 min. Afterward, cells were separately stained with monoclonal antibody recognizing JEV E, ZIKV NS5, DENV NS5, and PPV VP2 (4 ng/μl for fluorescence assay, prepared by our laboratory) for 1 h. Then the cells were washed three times with PBS and incubated with Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated secondary antibody (Invitrogen) for 30 min. The staining results were observed using a fluorescence microscope (Zeiss). HEK-293T cells were infected with BZM-CDs-treated-AAV at 50,000 MOI. At 24 h post-infection, the Green fluorescent protein (GFP) signals were observed using a fluorescence microscope (Zeiss).

Virus was collected from the supernatants of infected cells and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the cell debris. Cell supernatants were ultra-filtered by using a 0.22 μm filter and subjected onto a 30% to 60% (w/v) discontinuous sucrose gradient ultracentrifugation at 27,000 rpm for 4 h to obtain the virus resuspended in PBS. Then the virus concentrate was loaded onto a 30% (w/v) sucrose density and centrifuged at 27,000 rpm for 4 h. Viral particles were collected and dialyzed against PBS. The purified virus was incubated with BZM-CDs or DMEM at 37 °C for 1 h. The mixture was dropped on a copper grid for 15 min and negatively stained with phosphotungstic acid (PTA, pH = 7.0). The drying copper grids were observed by an electron microscope (Tecnai G2 20 TWIN).

All experiments were performed at least three times with similar conditions. Analyses were conducted using GraphPad Prism Software (version 7). Data were expressed as the mean ± SEM. Statistical differences among the experimental groups were determined using two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with subsequent Student’s test. For all tests, a P values of <0.05 was considered significant.

The BZM-CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal treatment of BZM in the presence of NaOH (Fig. 1 A). In the hydrothermal process, BZM might suffer the pyrolysis, carbonization and oxidization to form BZM-CDs, which were similar to lots of previous reports [41,43,44]. The morphology and size of BZM-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1B, the BZM-CDs were all well dispersedly spherical nanoparticles with small size (<10 nm) and a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm (Fig. 1B inset), which resolved the lattice spacing corresponded to the (1 0 0) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon [45]. Additionally, the calculated size histograms indicated that BZM-CDs had a narrow size distribution range from 3 to 6 nm with the mean size of 4.4 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 1C). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image also revealed that the BZM-CDs (Fig. S1) were small nanoparticles with sub-10 nm height. The histogram of particle size obtained by DLS in Fig. S2 confirmed that BZM-CDs had a distribution of diameter around 6.46 nm. This size was larger than that observed by TEM and AFM, which might be attributed to the hydration effect in water [46].

XRD was measured to identify the crystallinity of BZM-CDs and the result was shown in Fig. S3. A broad diffraction peak (2θ) was observed at around 22.9°, which was similar to previous CDs [47]. The Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of BZM-CDs was characterized to identify their functional groups and showed in Fig. 1D. It was obvious that BZM-CDs displayed two peaks at 3373 and 3168 cm−1, which were attributed to OH/NH. Moreover, CDs had a peak at 1600 cm−1, which was assigned to CO in amide groups. The peak at about 1398 cm−1 was attributed to COO−. The BZM-CDs were also characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to identify their surface chemical state. Fig. 2 A described that they had three peaks at 284 eV, 399 eV and 532 eV, which were ascribed to C, N, and O elements. The atomic ratio of C, N, O was 75.85%:5.09%:19.06%, suggesting the successful doping of nitrogen in BZM-CDs. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) could be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 284.5, 286.2 and 288.8 eV, corresponding to the CC/CC, CO/CN and CO bands, respectively [48]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S4A) had two peaks at 400.0 and 401.8 eV, which were attributed to CNC and NH groups [40,47]. The O 1s core level peak could be resolved into two components centered at 532.4 and 533.4 eV (Fig. S4B), which were associated with CO and COH/COC bands, respectively. Thus, the BZM-CDs processed both carboxylate and amide/amino groups.

The optical properties of the BZM-CDs were also explored. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum was demonstrated in Fig. 2C and showed that the BZM-CDs had peaks at 269 and 329 nm, which were caused by π → π* and n → π* transition [49]. The aqueous solution of BZM-CDs (inset in Fig. 2C) exhibited green luminescence under UV lamp (365 nm). Furthermore, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of BZM-CDs with various excitation wavelengths were also showed in Fig. 2C. When the excitation wavelength increased from 360 to 400 nm, the wavelength of the maximum emission of the BZM-CDs shifted from 482 to 502 nm, which was similar to other reported carbon dots and revealed the successful synthesis of BZM-CDs [50,51]. Furthermore, the representative 2D excitation-emission contour map of BZM-CDs was demonstrated in Fig. S5 and showed that BZM-CDs had one luminescent center at 493 nm under 380 nm excitation [52,53]. The photoluminescence QY of as-prepared BZM-CDs was calculated to be 13.1% (Table S1), which was comparable to recent reports [42,54–56]. The effects of pH, ion strength and continuous light irradiation on BZM-CDs were important facts for their biological applications. The emission spectra of BZM-CDs at different pH (3–11) were measured at 380 nm excitation (Fig. S6A) and indicated that pH had obvious influence on the fluorescence properties of BZM-CDs. The corresponding histogram of emission peak intensity (Fig. S6B) showed that the PL intensity of BZM-CDs had no significant change in the pH range of 5–9. The PL intensity decreased when pH was below 5 and had a slight increase as pH value increased from 9 to 11. As showed in Fig. S7, the ion strength did not cause obvious decrease of the fluorescence of BZM-CDs even at 0.5 M of NaCl. The PL intensity of BZM-CDs in Fig. S8 kept stable under sustaining light irradiation (380 nm) for 2 h, suggesting an excellent photostability of BZM-CDs. The zeta potential of BZM-CDs was 0.124 ± 0.026 eV in pH = 7.4 condition, which was due to abundant carboxylate and amide/amino groups on the surface.

In order to determine a suitable concentration of BZM-CDs for a biological study, we examined the effect of different concentrations of BZM-CDs (9.375–75 μg ml−1) on the viability of cultured BHK-21 and Vero cells by employing a luminescence-based cell viability assay. This assay determines the number of viable cells based on the quantification of ATP present, which indicates the presence of metabolically active cells. The results revealed that BZM-CDs exhibited no obvious cytotoxic effect under all experimental concentrations tested (Fig. 2D).

To explore the effect of BZM-CDs on flaviviral infections, we first measured the infectivity of JEV (P3 strain) by plaque reduction assay in the presence or absence of BZM-CDs. BHK-21 cells were infected with 200 plaque formation unit (PFU) of JEV treated with or without different concentrations (9.375–75 μg ml−1) of BZM-CDs. Our results revealed that the treatment of BZM-CDs prevented the viral infectivity in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 3 A and B). In contrast, the non-treated-JEV infected cells showed typical cytopathic effects (Fig. 3A). Additionally, the immunoblot analysis of BZM-CDs-treated-JEV infected cells was consistent with the plaque reduction assay (Fig. 3C and D). To investigate the inhibitory effect of BZM-CDs whether was dependent on time, BHK-21 cells were infected with 2000 PFU of JEV treated with 75 μg ml−1 of BZM-CDs for different time periods (10, 30, 60, and 90 min), and the JEV infectivity was measured by the plaque reduction assay. Fig. 3E displayed that the vast majority of virions lost their infectivity after incubating with BZM-CDs for 10 min, and no plaque was observed upon 60 min treatment. Furthermore, the inhibitory effect of BZM-CDs on the proliferation of JEV was assessed by one-step growth curve assay. When compared with the control group, the virus titers of BZM-CDs-treated group were found to be reduced (Fig. 3F). Collectively, these results suggested the potential role of BZM-CDs in blocking the viral infection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antiviral function of BZM-CDs is broad-spectrum
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the inhibitory effect of BZM-CDs on JEV infection observed in our study and a shared host-cell entry mechanism between JEV and other flaviviruses, we speculated that these BZM-CDs might also exert infection-blocking activity against other flaviviruses. Similar to the result for JEV, the treatment of BZM-CDs significantly reduced the ability of ZIKV to infect the cultured Vero cells as assessed by immunofluorescence assay (Fig. 4 A and B). Since ZIKV infection of Vero cells induced typical cytopathic effects, plaque reduction assay was employed to investigate the effect of BZM-CDs against ZIKV. Interestingly, BZM-CDs also caused a dose-dependent manner inhibitory effect on the ZIKV infectivity (Fig. S9). Furthermore, the proliferation of ZIKV was also found to be reduced by BZM-CDs as assessed by one-step growth curve assay (Fig. S10). Similarly, the BZM-CDs-associated inhibitory effect was examined during the DENV infection of cultured Vero cells by using the immunofluorescence assay, and the results were concordant to those observed during JEV and ZIKV infection of cultured cells (Fig. 4A and B).

Since flaviviruses belong to the enveloped class of viruses, non-enveloped viruses such as porcine parvovirus (PPV) and adenovirus-associated virus (AAV) were further used to explore the broad-spectrum infection-blocking potential of BZM-CDs. Interestingly, immunofluorescence analysis showed that the proportion of cells infected with BZM-CDs-treated-PPV- or -AAV was reduced markedly compared to the cells infected with non-treated viruses (Fig. 4C and D). The EC50 of BZM-CDs against different viruses was 18.63 μg ml−1 (JEV), 3.715 μg ml−1 (ZIKV), 37.49 μg ml−1 (DENV), 40.25 μg ml−1 (AAV), and 45.51 μg ml−1 (PPV). Notably, JEV and ZIKV were found to be more sensitive to BZM-CDs treatment compared to DENV and non-enveloped viruses. As JEV and ZIKV are structurally similar to each other [57], the observed fact might be associated with the different structure of the virus. Taken together, these results demonstrated the broad-spectrum therapeutic potential of BZM-CDs possibly by hindering the ability of viruses to infect the host-cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BZM-CDs directly interact with virus particles and block the viral infectivity
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that various biological surfaces of life structures can interact with CDs [31–33]. Therefore, we used JEV as a model virus to determine whether the observed reduction in viral infectivity was caused by a direct interaction between BZM-CDs and virus particles or by an indirect response of infected cells. Firstly, we compared the viral titer to the relationship of treatment of cells and virion with BZM-CDs. BHK-21 cells or virus were either pretreated or untreated with BZM-CDs for 1 h at 37 °C prior to JEV infection, and the subsequent impact on the viral titers were calculated by plaque assay. Interestingly, it was observed that treatment of cells with BZM-CDs did not clearly reduce the viral titers than virion-treated-group (Fig. 5 A). Thus, it was suggested that virions might be directly affected by BZM-CDs. To further demonstrate that BZM-CDs directly affected virus particles, TEM was used to observe the image of BZM-CDs and virion. We found that JEV P3 virions incubated in DMEM at 37 °C for 1 h retained their spherical morphology, whereas virions incubated with BZM-CDs had been absorbed by the BZM-CD particles (Fig. 5B). Hence, these findings suggested that the reduction of viral infectivity occurred due to a direct interaction of BZM-CDs with viral particles rather than the host-cells mounting an antiviral response.

The potential mechanism of action of CDs against viruses is poorly known. We set out to determine whether the materials were blocking a pre- or post-entry step in the viral life cycle; therefore, the phase of the JEV life cycle inhibited by BZM-CDs was next assessed. Since viral entry is dependent on the binding of virus particles to host-cells [4], a cell-binding assay using the BHK-21 cells was conducted. Briefly, JEV particles were preincubated with either 75 μg ml−1 of BMZ-CDs or with the equal volume of DMEM at 37 °C for 1 h. Then BHK-21 cells were incubated with the viral mixture at 4 °C for 1 h. After virus absorption, the cells were washed for three time to remove the non-adherent mixture, and the viral titers in the cell lysates were detected by plaque uniform assay. The results displayed that viral-binding to the host-cell was significantly reduced upon the treatment of JEV with BZM-CDs (Fig. 5C), suggesting that BZM-CDs affected the cell-binding capability of viral particles. In summary, these results demonstrated that pre-entry step of viral life cycle was blocked by BZM-CDs, which might account for the decrease in viral infectivity.

In summary, our study provided a new evidence that the biocompatible carbon dots (CDs) derived from benzoxazine monomers could avert the viral infectivity by directly interacting with virions, thus suggesting that CDs might limit the transmission of virus. Despite that some previous studies have demonstrated the potential antiviral feature of CDs [15,34–36], no solid evidence was provided that a direct interaction of CDs with viral particles lead to the reduction of viral infectivity, which was demonstrated in this study by plaque uniform assay and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Moreover, the present study demonstrated the first evidence of broad-spectrum antiviral activity of CDs derived from benzoxazine. We deduce that the approach given here is intrinsically broad-spectrum, which permits the potential prevention of multiple viral infections by using a single drug. Our developed approach displays a great advantage in virology field, where unexpected viral infections occur rapidly. Further detailed in vivo experiments are needed to determine whether prophylactic or therapeutic uses of the developed nanoparticles will be suitable.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFD0500407), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31825025, 31502065, 21773078), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2662016Q003, 2662015PY083, 2662015PY163, 2662016QD028) and the open funds of the State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology (AMLKF201702). The authors thank Pei Zhang from The Core Facility and Technical Support, Wuhan Institute of Virology, for helping in production of TEM micrographs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.02.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
